advaita.rendering: Scattered across many desires (aneka-citta, "many-minded"), these beings are wholly enveloped by the net of delusion (moha-jala) — which is nothing other than avidya (nescience), functioning as a veil that conceals the unity of Atman and Brahman. Tightly clinging to sense-pleasures (kama-bhogesu prasaktah), they accumulate impurity (kalmasa) without reprieve. Thus they fall into foul hells such as Vaitarani — not by divine punishment, but by the inexorable logic of unexamined action lacking viveka (discrimination).
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Shankara: 'moha = aviveka = ajnana; that very [moha] is like a net on account of its covering nature.' Hell is the mechanical consequence of accumulated kalmasa, not the judgment of a personal God.

visistadvaita.rendering: Believing themselves fully self-sufficient — 'without the aid of the unseen Ishvara, I alone can accomplish everything' — these souls spin in the confusion of many minds (aneka-citta-vibhranta), entangled in moha's net. Dying in the midst of their striving, clinging to sense-enjoyment (kama-bhogesu prasaktah), they fall into the impure hell. Their error is ontological: denying that every act of will requires divine support (Ishvara-sahakara) is itself the root confusion.
visistadvaita.bhashya_anchor: Ramanuja: 'without the cooperation of Ishvara etc. as unseen cause, [thinking] I alone am capable of doing everything.' The vibhranta (confusion) is precisely this denial of divine participation in all action.

dvaita.rendering: Dispersed among countless lower impulses and sealed within the web of moha, these souls — whose very capacity for right action depends entirely on Hari's will — cling to sense-pleasure and plunge into foul hell. Their fall is not accidental: the jiva (individual soul) is by nature dependent (paratantra); to act as though self-sufficient is to invert the fundamental order of reality and so invite what Hari, the dispenser of karma-phala (fruits of action), ordains as the appropriate consequence.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: Madhva offered no direct commentary on this verse. Rendering extrapolated from Dvaita first principles: jiva-paratantrya (absolute dependence of the soul on Hari) and Hari as karma-phala-data.
dvaita.bhashya_status: absent — no Madhva commentary on this verse; rendering from school axioms only

suddhadvaitа.rendering: Those who, like a half-awakened fool (ardha-prabuddha), wrongly claim 'I am Brahman' without having received Krsna's prasada (grace) are not liberated but baked in the great net of hells (maha-naraka-jala), as the Brahmanda-Purana declares. Ignorant ones (durnah) fixated on sense-enjoyment fall into the impure hell. Only the soul drunk on Krsna's lila-prasada escapes this net; self-assertion without that grace is itself the supreme delusion.
suddhadvaitа.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha cites Brahmanda-Purana: 'the ignorant one and the half-awakened one who says I am Brahman — he is cooked in the great net of hells, there is no doubt.' The commentary is terse but the citation is structurally decisive.
suddhadvaitа.bhashya_status: attested — brief but with Brahmanda citation

bhakti.rendering: The mind that rushes into countless desires becomes 'many-minded' (aneka-citta); thus scattered, these beings are bewildered and wrapped in the net of moha — just as fish are trapped and controlled by a string-net (sutra-maya-jala). So bound, they press ever deeper into sense-enjoyment (kama-bhogesu abhinivistah) and fall into the foul, filth-filled hell (asuci = kashmala-naraka). The fish-image is vivid: they mistake the net for freedom because desire itself is the invisible cord.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Sridhara: 'like fish controlled by a string-net (sutra-maya-jala)... so bound, fallen into the foul (kashmala) hell.' The fish simile is Sridhara's own elaboration, absent in Shankara.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Scattered by many corrupt intentions (dustah-sankalpah) of the kinds already described, and thoroughly enclosed by the net of moha — moha being the incapacity to discriminate between beneficial and harmful (hita-ahita-vastu-viveka-asamarthya), which is a net precisely because it binds — these souls are made wholly subject to it, like fish enslaved by a string-net. Consequently they cling to even unwanted sense-pleasures, sin accumulating moment by moment, and fall into hells such as Vaitarani, filled with excrement, urine, and phlegm.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusudana: 'moha = incapacity to discriminate between beneficial and harmful... just as fish enslaved by a string-net'; hell described as 'filled with excrement, urine, and phlegm (vin-mutra-slesmadi).' Synthesis of Shankara's jnana-frame with Sridhara's fish image.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_status: attested — most expansive commentary in the panel

so_what_questions: What is the difference between having many interests and being 'many-minded' (aneka-citta) in the verse's sense — where does productive complexity become scattered delusion?
Both Shankara and Madhusudana use the term kalmasa (impurity/sin) accumulating gradually — what does it mean for moral degradation to be cumulative rather than event-based?
Ramanuja alone emphasises that the delusion includes denying Ishvara's cooperation in every act; how does the experience of self-sufficiency relate to spiritual bondage in your own life?
The fish-in-a-net image (Sridhara, Madhusudana) suggests the bound person cannot perceive the net from inside it — what practices across traditions are designed to make the net visible?
Vallabha cites the Brahmanda-Purana on the 'half-awakened' person who claims liberation prematurely; what distinguishes genuine awakening from its imitation?
Hell (naraka) here is described in visceral physical terms (Vaitarani, filth) — what is gained and what is lost by reading this as literal cosmology versus as a description of a psychological state?
The verse names two distinct mechanisms: scattered mind (aneka-citta) and the net of delusion (moha-jala) — are these sequential, or can moha operate even in a focused single-minded person?

everyday_applications.advaita: Notice when the mind is running five parallel desire-tracks at once — career advancement, social approval, comfort, status, entertainment — and recognise this aneka-citta not as ambition but as accumulated kalmasa preventing the viveka that alone clarifies what is real. The Advaita application is diagnostic: pause, identify which of the many 'cintas' are actually avidya in disguise.
everyday_applications.visistadvaita: Before beginning any project or decision, pause to acknowledge that the capacity to act at all is lent by Ishvara. Ramanuja's reading makes the practical instruction clear: the delusion that 'I alone can do this' is not modesty rhetoric but a category error about where agency originates. Treating every effort as kainkarya (service) rather than autonomous achievement is the corrective.
everyday_applications.dvaita: When you find yourself defending your choices as entirely self-generated ('I earned this, I built this'), check whether you are performing the inversion that Dvaita regards as the root confusion — treating the paratantra (dependent) soul as svatantra (independent). The practical discipline is regular acknowledgment of Hari as the actual agent behind every capacity you exercise.
everyday_applications.suddhadvaitа: Vallabha's warning targets the spiritual practitioner specifically: the person who has read enough to speak confidently about liberation but has not yet been softened by Krsna's prasada. The everyday application is to distrust one's own confident spiritual conclusions and remain open to the lila-prasada that arrives unexpectedly, not as a product of one's own effort.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's fish image offers a daily contemplative probe: in any activity dominated by wanting — scrolling, purchasing, planning — ask whether you can see the net or only the water. The string-net (sutra-maya-jala) is invisible from inside desire. A moment of genuine questioning ('what is this desire actually in service of?') is the first act of getting outside the net.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's definition of moha as 'incapacity to discriminate between beneficial and harmful' is a practical cognitive description, not only a metaphysical one. The everyday application: when facing a choice that feels urgent, check whether the urgency itself is evidence of hita-ahita-viveka-asamarthya — the inability to tell what actually serves you versus what serves the desire. Slowness and stillness before deciding are the practical antidote.

self_assessment: All 6 bhāṣyas present and used directly; Dvaita flagged absent with school-axiom extrapolation; Sridhara used Sanskrit content only (no HTML artifacts detected in payload); fish-net image preserved across Sridhara and Madhusudana with distinct emphases; Ramanuja's unique Ishvara-sahakara angle foregrounded as genuine doctrinal divergence.
